speaker: interviewer
text: --with Mr. Henry Blasenstein. It is July 14, 1996. So you were in America. You were working as a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Diamond polisher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your first wife passed away. Did there come a time that you married again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Two years later, in 1975, my wife-- I married my present wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name [? is--? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Eda.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your children were growing up, did you ever talk to them about your wartime experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little, mostly not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We couldn't-- couldn't talk about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because it's very hard to talk now about it. If you would tell me in-- in a month or six months to repeat all that, what I repeated now, I wouldn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever ask questions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Now, I'm gonna give them the tapes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you answer, when they asked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Used to give them some answers, you know, about the-- just like, in-- in few sentences, the whole story, you know? Especially my daughter-- she's-- she's-- she would like to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel that your experiences during the war-- did they affect you afterwards? Do they affect you even to this day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it affects me physically, you know. I-- I'm not in-- in good health. I have my problems, you know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It affects you, you know, family way. You know. I don't have no family. You know. See people-- lot of people cannot understand-- especially my grandchildren-- that they don't have no uncles, no aunts, no cousins. You know what I mean? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't think so-- even though my grandson is 16, and my-- my other two granddaughters, one is 12, one is 11. I don't know if they still understand all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you want to try to tell them? What message would you want to give to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like them to see the tape and find out [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what happened to their grandmother and their-- their grandfather. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Unfortunately, they cannot have a tape of their real grandmother, you know. But if they're going to hear it from Eda, from this great grandmother-- so they're going to get an idea of what happened to-- to their real grandmother. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any particular message you would want to tell them? Or advice you would want to give?

speaker: interviewee
text: But what can I tell them? Just remember. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] That they had a good grandmother and good grandfather. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They are always on-- on my mind. You know. And one day they understand that. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] Most important that they should be good people, good to other ones. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To remember what's happened to the Jewish people. [CRYING] This is something nobody can understand that. You do not-- you haven't-- [CRYING] It means to be 19 years old and be alone in the whole world. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] I admire people who still have some families, you know-- like some people [? found ?] they had brother still, or sister. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] Thank God, we have now a family. I've got a good wife and-- and stepchildren and my own grandchildren. They are all nice to each other. Everybody is nice. And that's what keeps you going. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I am now almost 71 years old. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you don't know how long you have to go, still. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Just pray to God to-- to be healthy. And by [? god, ?] if you can get up in the morning and still get dressed, that's a-- a big if-- if. I take-- I'm joined the-- no. I belonged to the temple for a long, long time. But I participate much more now in the temple than I did before, you know, when I used to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your experiences in the Holocaust affect how you feel about God?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] [? That, ?] I have mixed feelings. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because you have-- from one side, you thank God that you survived. So you-- you thank God that you survived. But from other side, there were people, you know, in Poland, that [? were Grand ?] [? Rabbis-- ?] you know, sages. You know. And God didn't take care of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you have mixed feeling, you know. For me, myself, I am grateful to God that I survived. But other ones, I don't think so. They are not alive. They-- they cannot-- they cannot say that. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Is there anything else you'd like to add, in closing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would like to live to see my grandchildren get married. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And now, we have a-- a new one who is only 14 month old, our grandchild. So I would like to see her grow up. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Who knows. Maybe I will live to see them-- to see them. You see, my first wife didn't live to see her daughter get married. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She didn't see her grandchildren. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This is my mother. That picture was taken in 1935. And actually, this is a part of a picture taken, you know, cut away from-- from another picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, and her name was Dora Wanderer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got that picture from our cousin, who was in Russia. And a cousin of mine was on that picture, too. So my son cut away the picture and blowed it up. Because it was a tiny little picture. And he blowed it up. That's why it's not so clear, you know. And that picture was very old. And that's how I had-- that's the only picture I have from before the war. That's it. Nothing else. This is the Schindler's list. And I am one of them. One of the 1,100 people from Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to get a copy of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I got a copy? Some kind of organization sent it in. You know, they were-- they were, you know, recording for the nation. You know. They didn't know that I-- that was one of the Schindler's list. I just happened to get it. Imagine 1,000 other people got it, too. This picture-- this picture was taken after the war, in [? Linz, ?] Austria. These were two-- two of my friends. The one on the left-- I forgot his name. And I don't know where he is, what happened to him. The one in the middle is Henry Sperling. He lives in Clark, New Jersey. And the one on the right is me. We just put on the-- our uniform, what we used to wear, during-- during the war, in camp. This is a picture of me, taken in Paris in 1948. I was 23 years old at the time. Handsome boy. [LAUGHS] This is a picture of my first wife with my two children. That picture was taken in 1955 in Vittel, France. We went on vacation that time there. And this is the house where we stayed. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And your children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: And children is Jacques and Lillian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Stephanie. This is a picture of my first wife. And we'd taken it shortly before she passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name was Stephanie. This is my son, Jacques. This picture was taken a-- recently-- a year or two ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in '92 on my grandson's Scott bar-- bar mitzvah. From left to right-- this is my granddaughter Pamela. Next-- next sitting down, am I with my wife, Eda. And in the back-- and next-- next next to her is my other granddaughter [? Andrea. ?] And in the back is my son-- son-in-law, [? Howl. ?] And-- and the bar mitzvah boy, Scott. Is there anybody else there? I don't think-- is there anybody else there? Yeah? I can't see it. Start from the beginning? Right behind me is my daughter Lillian.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken a few years ago. And this is my two granddaughters. The one on the left is Pamela. And the one on the right is Andrea. This is my grandson, Scott. That picture was taken on a prom, [INAUDIBLE]. It was taken about just three months ago. Or even less. This-- I am here with my wife, Eda. This picture was taken on one-- on one of our grandchildren's bar mitzvah. And I don't-- I don't remember which one. This picture was taken on-- on my grandson's bar mitzvah, in December of '92. In the front row, from left to right, is my grandson [? Brian, ?] the bar mitzvah boy, Scott. Down-- and I on it. And my wife Eda. Down is my granddaughter Pamela. That's my granddaughter Andrea. In the back-- in the back row is my son-in-law Mark, my daughter-in-law [? Sally, ?] my son Jacques, my daughter Lillian, my son-in-law [? Howl, ?] and my daughter-in-law [? Juliana, ?] and my son David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Blasenstein, would you like to introduce your family for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: To my-- to my left is my granddaughter Pamela. In the background to the left is my son-in-law [? Howl. ?] Next to him is my daughter Sally. Next, is my grandson [? Brian. ?] Next to [? Brian ?] is my daughter Lillian. Next to Lillian is my daughter-in-law [? Juliana. ?] And next to [? Juliana ?] is her husband, my son, David. And to the right of my wife, in front, is my granddaughter Andrea. And next to me is my wife Eda.

speaker: interviewee
text: And your lap?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And your lap?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [LAUGHTER] I forgot about the most important person. On my lap is my granddaughter--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Willow. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Willow. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would anybody like to add anything at this time? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Willow, ?] do you want to say something? [? Willow. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I can go out and write something. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Sally, you have anything to say? Or Lillian?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. DAVID (SON): I just think it's a good opportunity for survivors of the Holocaust to be able to say what's happened and tell their story. Because, you know, God knows there's not enough of them around. And pass it along to the next generation and the following generations. So this will never happen again. SALLY (DAUGHTER): I-- I also think it's very important to be able to pass it to future generations. I know that my own children really have very little knowledge of what's happened to their grandparents during the-- during the Holocaust. And I would like them to view the tape and have an idea of what they went through. And I thank you for making this possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you all very much. SALLY (DAUGHTER): Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We thank you very much.
